questions.question: A $5.00\text{ kg}$ ball accelerates from rest at $2.00\text{ m/s}^2$ for $1.50\text{ s}$. Calculate its momentum after the acceleration.

Two blocks undergo a sudden impact while sliding along a flat, level, and frictionless guide rail.

Which of the following conditions must be true regarding this interaction?
options: The total mechanical kinetic energy of the system remains perfectly constant
The net linear momentum of the entire system remains perfectly constant
The individual momentum of each block remains completely unaltered
The individual velocity of each block remains completely unaltered
questions.explanation: In any collision on a frictionless surface with no external forces, total momentum is always conserved. Kinetic energy is only conserved in elastic collisions. Individual momenta and velocities change during impact.

questions.question: A $2.00\text{ kg}$ object starts from rest and reaches a final momentum of $30.0\text{ kg m/s}$ under a constant net force of $10.0\text{ N}$. How long did the force act?
An object accelerates from rest at $3.00\text{ m/s}^2$ for $2.50\text{ s}$ and reaches a final momentum of $45.0\text{ kg m/s}$. What is its mass?
A $4.00\text{ kg}$ ball changes its velocity from $2.00\text{ m/s}$ to $8.00\text{ m/s}$ in $3.00\text{ s}$. What average force acted on it?
A $2.50\text{ kg}$ object has a momentum of $20.0\text{ kg m/s}$ after accelerating for $4.00\text{ s}$ from rest. What was its acceleration?
A $6.00\text{ kg}$ cart experiences a constant force of $15.0\text{ N}$ for $2.00\text{ s}$, resulting in a final momentum of $78.0\text{ kg m/s}$. What was its initial velocity?
A $10.0\text{ kg}$ object moving at $5.00\text{ m/s}$ experiences an opposing force of $20.0\text{ N}$ for $1.50\text{ s}$. What is its final momentum?
questions.explanation: $p_{initial} = 10.0 \times 5.00 = 50.0\text{ kg m/s}$. $\Delta p = -20.0 \times 1.50 = -30.0\text{ kg m/s}$. $p_{final} = 50.0 - 30.0 = 20.0\text{ kg m/s}$.

questions.question:
An empty mining cart of mass $M$ coasts down a horizontal rail line at a constant speed of $3.60\text{ m/s}$ until it impacts and couples with a stationary loaded cart of mass $3M$.

What is the final speed of the joined carts immediately after the collision?

Two skaters, Nadia and Owen, initially at rest, push away from one another on a smooth, icy surface. Nadia has a mass of $45\text{ kg}$ and Owen has a mass of $75\text{ kg}$. After they separate, Nadia moves away with a speed of $4\text{ m/s}$.

As the skaters glide apart, Owen has
questions.explanation: Momentum is equal and opposite ($180\text{ kg m/s}$ each). Owen is heavier, so his velocity is lower ($2.4\text{ m/s}$). $KE_N = \frac{1}{2} \times 45 \times 4^2 = 360\text{ J}$. $KE_O = \frac{1}{2} \times 75 \times 2.4^2 = 216\text{ J}$. Owen has less kinetic energy.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered the connections between force, acceleration, time, and momentum!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review using $F \times t = \Delta p$ as a shortcut for finding momentum change.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on $v = u + at$, $p = mv$, and $F = \Delta p / t$.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: $F = ma$, $v = u + at$, $p = mv$, and the impulse-momentum relationship $F \times t = \Delta p$.
